summary: Gruber dismantles a Reuters report that casually states Apple 'ceded its crown to Samsung in 2012,' arguing this narrative is factually unsupported. He identifies three bad assumptions that observers developed during Apple's period of unchallenged smartphone dominance: that Apple should have overwhelming market share, that its success was solely due to lack of competition, and that real competition would doom the iPhone. He demonstrates that Apple never led in market share but commands 70% of handset industry profits, while Samsung holds the number two spot by every metric except units sold. Gruber contends the media is declaring Apple's fall not because evidence supports it, but because journalists are bored with the existing narrative and eager to write a new one.
key_points: Apple never had or pursued smartphone market share dominance — Steve Jobs's original goal was just 1% of the total phone market
Apple captured 72% of handset industry profits in Q4 2012 while Samsung took 29%, with every other manufacturer losing money
Three flawed assumptions drove the Apple-is-losing narrative: market share expectations, attributing success solely to lack of competition, and believing Apple can't actually compete
Samsung's achievement, while remarkable, represents the number two spot behind Apple by every measurable metric except unit sales
The media's 'mighty Apple has fallen' narrative is being stated as fact despite contradicting evidence, driven by desire for a new story rather than data
money_quotes: The desire for the 'Oh, how the mighty Apple has fallen' narrative is so strong that the narrative is simply being stated as fact, evidence to the contrary be damned.

They're declaring 'The King is dead; long live the King' not because the king has actually died or abdicated the throne, but because they're bored with the king and want to write a new coronation story.
But 'iPhone would do better with no serious competition', which is true, is very different from 'iPhone would falter if it had any serious competition', which is false.
Apple and Samsung have together destroyed the rest of the mobile handset industry.
That's a statement of fact, in a Reuters news (not opinion) story, about a company with 70 percent (and judging by last quarter, growing) of the industry's profits.
An overwhelming majority market share has never been Apple's plan for the iPhone. A hope, perhaps. But not the plan.
The fact that Apple and the iPhone now face at least one serious (and successful) competitor somehow means that the iPhone is already losing. This is insane.
key_insight: The media narrative that Apple has lost its smartphone crown to Samsung is stated as fact but contradicted by every metric except unit market share, which Apple never led or prioritized.
